_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Laclede County, MO. Record survival and courthouse history. Laclede County formed 1849. No major courthouse fire — records are relatively well-preserved from county formation. Lebanon is the county seat.

previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced as county grew. Records transferred.
Replaced by the current 1925 building. Records transferred intact.

record_losses.cause: Civil War guerrilla warfare disrupted record-keeping; some records incomplete during war years
records_survived: Most county records survived; courthouse not burned
record_losses.description: The Civil War disrupted record-keeping in Laclede County. Guerrilla activity made court sessions irregular and dangerous. Some records from the war years are incomplete or missing. The courthouse itself was not burned — unusual for a Missouri Ozark county in the guerrilla zone.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Records from 1861-1865 may have gaps — the guerrilla war made holding court dangerous and recording transactions secondary to survival.
record_losses.source: Missouri State Archives. Guide to County Records on Microfilm: Laclede County.

deeds.gaps: Civil War-era recording may be incomplete.
deeds.repository: Laclede County Recorder of Deeds, Lebanon; Missouri State Archives

wills_estates.gaps: Sparse in earliest years; few estates to probate on the frontier.
wills_estates.repository: Laclede County Probate Court; Missouri State Archives

marriages.repository: Laclede County Recorder; Missouri State Archives

court_minutes.completeness: Good — from county formation with Civil War-era gaps.
court_minutes.gaps: Sessions irregular during the Civil War.
court_minutes.repository: Missouri State Archives; Laclede County Circuit Court

vital_records.completeness: Poor compliance in rural areas until approximately 1910. Lebanon compliance better than the surrounding hollows.
vital_records.repository: Missouri Department of Health and Senior Services; Missouri State Archives

substitute_sources.description: The Laclede County Republican and its successor papers documented births, deaths, marriages, court proceedings, and community events from the 1870s.
substitute_sources.repository: State Historical Society of Missouri; Laclede County Library
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Good supplement to courthouse records. Court session reporting, property transfers, and obituaries are particularly useful.
substitute_sources.source: State Historical Society of Missouri newspaper holdings.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1850-1940. First enumerated as Laclede County in 1850.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential baseline. The 1860 census is particularly valuable for documenting the pre-war population in a county that was heavily disrupted by guerrilla warfare.

substitute_sources.description: Motor courts, cafes, and gas stations along Route 66 through Lebanon kept business records that may document employment and community members in the highway era.
substitute_sources.repository: Laclede County Historical Society; Route 66 Association of Missouri
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Limited but occasionally useful for tracking families in the Route 66 economy.

narrative_hooks: Laclede County's courthouse survived the Civil War intact — unusual in the Missouri Ozarks. If {ancestor_name} was in the county from 1849, the deed and marriage records are there.
Missouri vital records were required from 1883 but rarely enforced in the hollows — if {ancestor_name} was born in a cabin east of Lebanon, the birth certificate may never have been filed.

The 1860 census is the last snapshot of Laclede County before the guerrilla war tore it apart — if {ancestor_name} was enumerated that year, the census may be the last normal record before four years of chaos.
The Laclede County Republican documented the community from the 1870s — if {ancestor_name} died or appeared in court, the newspaper may hold the notice.
source_references: Missouri State Archives. Guide to County Records on Microfilm: Laclede County.
State Historical Society of Missouri newspaper holdings.
Works Progress Administration. Historical Records Survey: Missouri County Records. Jefferson City, 1937-1942.
